musicalLanguage.description: This transcription of Debussy's Arabesque No. 1 runs 106 measures at 93 BPM in 4/4 time with 1,392 notes, and its key picture is genuinely unsettled: the stated E major fits the content only weakly (statedKeyFit -0.102), and content-based detection is too ambiguous to trust either (0.04 margin, below the analysis's own confidence floor). Rather than force a single tonic, the honest reading leans on the implied chord windows themselves, which scatter across many different roots without settling on one: Em7 and G major tie as the most-cited labels (4 windows apiece, 3.4% of measures considered), with Am, Bm, D#, Em, and F close behind. What the data does establish cleanly is the piece's architecture: the opening material (the right hand's measures 2-16, the left hand's measures 5-18) returns almost verbatim near the close (measures 72-86 and 75-88), a genuine recapitulation confirmed by two independent pattern groups plus several byte-identical single-measure pairs (10 and 80, 14 and 84, 15 and 85 among them).
musicalLanguage.structure: Eleven structural regions from the section-lens pass, the two sharpest boundaries opening at measure 76 (novelty 5.883) and measure 6 (5.803) — the return of the opening material and the piece's very first structural turn. The recapitulation is the piece's defining structural fact: a 102-note left-hand span (measures 5-18) restates almost exactly at measures 75-88 (moderate evidence, coverage 0.307), and a 79-note right-hand span (measures 2-16) returns the same way at measures 72-86 (coverage 0.277) — independently corroborated by single measures 10, 14, and 15 each matching their counterparts 80, 84, and 85 byte-for-byte. Inside that outer frame, a 13-note right-hand figure recurs three times (measures 17-18, 71-72, 87-88), and a 14-note right-hand cell recurs twice more, transposed, near measures 63-65 and 101-103 — the same rolling triplet-arpeggio material threading through both the opening and closing pages.
keyMoments: The recapitulation at measures 72-88 restates the opening material from measures 2-18 almost exactly, confirmed independently by two pattern groups and three byte-identical measure pairs (10/80, 14/84, 15/85) — a textbook example of how a return of the opening theme creates large-scale unity even in a piece as fluid as this one.

The stated E major fits the content only weakly (statedKeyFit -0.102) and content detection cannot confidently name a replacement key either — this teaches an important listening skill: not every tonal piece anchors as firmly to its stated key as its title suggests, and the ear must sometimes follow color rather than a single tonic.

The implied Em7 and G major windows tie as the most frequently cited harmonic colors, with G major sitting a whole step below the stated E major's dominant — a reminder that Debussy's early harmonic language floats between closely related centers rather than locking onto one.

A 13-note right-hand figure recurring at measures 17-18, 71-72, and 87-88 demonstrates a single rolling triplet shape doing structural work across three widely separated points in the piece — recognizing it once teaches all three appearances at once.
teachingGoals: Practice the opening material (measures 2-18) and its recapitulation (measures 72-88) side by side — learning them as the same passage, not two different sections, builds the piece far faster.

Listen for the implied Em7 and G major colors recurring throughout, and develop comfort with a piece whose harmony floats between centers rather than confirming one tonic the way a Classical-era piece would.
Recognize the 13-note right-hand triplet figure at measures 17-18, 71-72, and 87-88 as one repeated shape — practice it once and apply it at all three locations.
Build evenness in the triplet arpeggios throughout, since the piece's flowing character depends on the sixteenth-note and triplet figuration staying smooth rather than metronomic or heavy.
styleTips: Let the triplets shimmer rather than pound — listen for the rolling right-hand figures staying light even as they recur across the recapitulation at measures 72-88.
Pedal generously but change with each shift in the implied harmony (Em7, G major, Am, Bm, D#) — listen for the color changing smoothly as the analysis's chord windows move between roots.
Balance the melody so it floats on top of the arpeggiated texture rather than getting buried in it — listen for the top line staying audible through the busiest triplet passages.
Treat the recapitulation at measures 72-88 as a return, not a repeat — listen for it carrying the memory of everything played since the opening, even though the notes are nearly identical.
